---
title: "Why Statistical Learning?"
subtitle: "Week 1 — Motivation, Importance, and How It Works (ISLP Ch. 1–2)"
author: "Hyun-Hwan Jeong"
date: 2026-07-10
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.08
    slide-number: c/t
    center: true
    transition: fade
    footer: "ML Summer Class · Week 1"
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    html-math-method: katex
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
---

# Welcome {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

::: notes
Quick intros, logistics (Fridays 2-4pm Central, 7 weeks), and set expectations:
read before class, we spend class time on discussion + labs.
:::

## About this course

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Instructors:** Ho-min Park & Hyun-Hwan Jeong
- **This session:** led by Hyun-Hwan Jeong
- **Format:** 7 Fridays, 2:00–4:00 PM Central, Jul 10 – Aug 21
- **Textbook:** *An Introduction to Statistical Learning*, Python edition (ISLP)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Read the chapter **before** class
- Class time = discussion + labs, not lecture
- Every week pairs theory with a runnable notebook
:::
::::

::: notes
Emphasize the flipped-classroom structure — the condensed 7-week pace only
works if people show up having read the chapter.
:::

## Course syllabus

| # | Date | Topics | Chapters |
|---|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Jul 10 | Intro + Statistical Learning basics | Ch 1–2 |
| 2 | Jul 17 | Linear Regression; Classification | Ch 3–4 |
| 3 | Jul 24 | Resampling; Regularization | Ch 5–6 |
| 4 | Jul 31 | Moving Beyond Linearity; Tree-Based Methods | Ch 7–8 |
| 5 | Aug 7 | Support Vector Machines; Deep Learning I | Ch 9, Ch 10 (pt 1) |
| 6 | Aug 14 | Deep Learning II; Unsupervised Learning | Ch 10 (pt 2), Ch 12 |
| 7 | Aug 21 | Survival Analysis; Multiple Testing; wrap-up | Ch 11, Ch 13 |

Full reading list and slide decks for every week live in the repo README.

# Why Are We Doing This? {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Data is everywhere — decisions aren't automatically good

[Every field now collects more data than any person can look at by eye.]{.hl}
The bottleneck has shifted from *collecting* data to *turning it into
decisions*. That's exactly the gap statistical learning fills.

::: {.fragment}
> "Statistical learning refers to a vast set of tools for understanding data."
> — James, Witten, Hastie, Tibshirani, Taylor (ISLP, Ch. 1)
:::

## Wage data — a regression problem

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig1-1-wage.png" alt=""></div>
```

Predict a worker's **wage** — a continuous number — from age, education,
and year. We want to estimate it as accurately as possible.

## Stock market data — a classification problem

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig1-2-stock-market.png" alt=""></div>
```

Predict whether the market goes **up or down** tomorrow — a category, not
a number — from the past few days' returns.

## Gene expression data — no target at all

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig1-4-gene-expression.png" alt=""></div>
```

No output to predict — just **structure** hiding in thousands of
measurements. Colors mark the true cancer type; the method never saw
those labels, yet the clusters line up with them anyway.

## One shape, three questions

Regression, classification, clustering: different questions, but the same
underlying machinery — observe data, estimate structure, check how well
the estimate generalizes.

::: {.fragment}
That machinery is what this course teaches.
:::

## This isn't a fad — it's ~200 years in the making

| | |
|---|---|
| **Early 1800s** | Legendre & Gauss formalize **least squares** — the ancestor of linear regression |
| **1936** | Fisher's **linear discriminant analysis** for classification |
| **1940s–70s** | Logistic regression, **generalized linear models** |
| **1980s** | Breiman et al.: **classification and regression trees** |
| **1990s** | **Support vector machines**; the term *statistical learning* emerges |
| **2010s–now** | Deep learning, huge data, cheap compute — old ideas at new scale |

::: {.fragment}
Almost everything in this course is a 20th-century statistical idea, made
newly practical by 21st-century data and compute.
:::

## Why *you*, specifically

- These methods show up **inside** the tools you already use: recommendation
  systems, medical risk scores, fraud detection, A/B tests, genomics
  pipelines.
- Being able to read a model's assumptions (and know when they break) is
  what separates *using* a black box from *trusting* one.
- The skills transfer directly to the Python data-science stack
  (`scikit-learn`, `statsmodels`) you'll use outside this course.

# Why It Is Important {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## It's the common language of modern data work

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
- **Science** — genomics, astrophysics, climate models
- **Medicine** — diagnosis support, survival/risk prediction
- **Business** — demand forecasting, pricing, churn
- **Technology** — search ranking, spam filtering, the models behind LLMs
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{=html}
<div class="stat-card">
  <i class="fa-solid fa-diagram-project"></i>
  <div>
    <strong>One toolkit, many domains.</strong>
    <p>The same bias-variance trade-off that governs a linear regression
    also governs a neural network. Learn the trade-off once.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```
:::
::::

## Getting it wrong is expensive

- An **overfit** model looks great on the data you trained it on and fails
  the moment it meets new data — silently, until it's in production.
- An **overly rigid** model misses real signal and gives you false
  confidence in a simple story.
- Statistical learning gives you the *vocabulary and diagnostics*
  (train/test split, cross-validation, bias-variance) to tell which
  mistake you're making — and fix it.

::: notes
This is the seed for Chapter 2's bias-variance discussion and Chapter 5's
cross-validation — plant the idea now, they'll see the math for it soon.
:::

# How It Works {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## First, the vocabulary

The book's running example: the **Advertising** data — `sales` of a product
in $n = 200$ markets, alongside `TV`, `radio`, and `newspaper` ad budgets.

- What we predict **from** — the budgets — are the **inputs**: also called
  [predictors]{.hl}, *features*, or *independent variables* ($X_1, X_2, X_3$)
- What we predict — `sales` — is the **output**: the [response]{.hl} or
  *dependent variable* ($Y$)
- **Notation for the whole course:** $n$ = number of observations,
  $p$ = number of predictors, $x_{ij}$ = predictor $j$ for observation $i$

::: {.fragment}
The book (and every paper you'll ever read) uses these names
interchangeably — hear "feature," think "predictor," think $X$.
:::

## The basic setup

We assume there's some relationship between an input $X$ and an output $Y$:

$$Y = f(X) + \epsilon$$

::: {.fragment}
- $f$ — the **true, unknown** systematic relationship we want to learn
- $\epsilon$ — random noise, independent of $X$, mean zero
- **Statistical learning = a set of approaches for estimating $f$**
:::

::: {.fragment}
Everything this course covers — linear regression, trees, neural networks —
is a different strategy for estimating the same $f$.
:::

## Two reasons to estimate $f$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Prediction
We only care about the output. $f$ can be a "black box" as long as
$\hat{Y} = \hat{f}(X)$ is accurate.

*e.g. will this loan default?*
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Inference
We want to understand **how** $Y$ changes with $X$: which predictors
matter, and how. $f$ must stay interpretable.

*e.g. which drug dosage actually helps?*
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
Most real projects are a mix of both — and the mix determines which method
you should reach for.
:::

## Two kinds of error — only one is your fault

Even a *perfect* estimate of $f$ would not predict perfectly:

$$E(Y - \hat{Y})^2 = \underbrace{\left[f(X) - \hat{f}(X)\right]^2}_{\text{reducible}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\epsilon)}_{\text{irreducible}}$$

::: {.fragment}
- **Reducible error** shrinks as $\hat{f}$ improves — every method in this
  course is a strategy for reducing it.
- **Irreducible error** comes from unmeasured variables and inherent
  randomness. [No model, however clever, gets below it.]{.hl}
- In practice that floor is *unknown* — a model that plateaus leaves you
  guessing whether you've hit the floor or just a bad $\hat{f}$.
:::

::: notes
This equation is the seed for the bias-variance decomposition later in the
deck — Var(ε) will reappear there as the third, untouchable term.
:::

## How do we estimate $f$?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Parametric methods
1. Assume a functional **form** (e.g. $f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$)
2. Estimate a small set of **parameters** from the training data

Simpler, needs less data, but wrong if the assumed form is wrong.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Non-parametric methods
No explicit functional form — let the data determine the shape of $f$
directly (e.g. splines, trees, KNN).

More flexible, needs more data, harder to interpret.
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
Every method in this course sits somewhere on this spectrum — and where it
sits determines how much data it needs and how easy it is to explain.
:::

## The flexibility spectrum, mapped

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig2-7-flexibility-interpretability.png" alt=""></div>
```

Every method in this course has a home on this plot. Moving right buys
flexibility at the cost of interpretability — there's no free lunch.

## Two kinds of learning problems

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Supervised
Every observation has a **labeled outcome** $Y$. We fit $f$ to match
inputs to known answers.

Regression (numeric $Y$) · Classification (categorical $Y$)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Unsupervised
No labeled outcome — only inputs $X$. We look for structure: groups,
patterns, lower-dimensional summaries.

Clustering · Dimension reduction
:::
::::

## Regression vs. classification

- $Y$ **quantitative** (a number: wage, price, temperature) → a
  **regression** problem
- $Y$ **qualitative** (a category: default/no-default, up/down) → a
  **classification** problem

::: {.fragment}
The distinction matters because we measure "getting it right"
differently — a wrong number is off by *some amount*; a wrong category is
just *wrong*. That difference drives which methods and which accuracy
metrics apply.
:::

## Measuring the quality of fit

For regression, the standard yardstick is **mean squared error**:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{f}(x_i)\right)^2$$

::: {.fragment}
- Small on the **training data** is easy — and not the point.
- What we actually care about is small **test MSE**: how well $\hat{f}$
  predicts *new*, unseen observations.
- A model can look excellent on training data and terrible on test
  data — that gap is overfitting, made precise.
:::

## The bias-variance trade-off, precisely

Expected test error at a point $x_0$ decomposes into three pieces:

$$E\left[\left(y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0)\right)^2\right] = \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \left[\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(x_0))\right]^2 + \text{Var}(\epsilon)$$

- **Variance** — how much $\hat{f}$ would change on a different training
  set. Flexible models (trees, splines, deep nets) vary a lot.
- **Bias** — error from approximating a complex reality with a simpler
  model. Simple models (linear regression, LDA) tend to be biased.
- $\text{Var}(\epsilon)$ — irreducible noise. No model beats it.

::: {.fragment}
Minimizing test error means *trading* variance against bias — not
eliminating either one.
:::

## What it looks like in practice

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig2-12-bias-variance.png" alt=""></div>
```

Squared bias (cyan) falls and variance (orange) rises as flexibility
increases. Test MSE (red) is their sum — it bottoms out in the middle,
not at either extreme.

::: notes
Worth pausing here — this U-shape is the single most important picture in
Chapter 2, and it recurs (in disguise) in cross-validation curves,
regularization paths, and learning curves for the rest of the course.
:::

## The classification setting

Instead of MSE, classification uses the **error rate**:

$$\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} I(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i)$$

::: {.fragment}
The theoretical best possible classifier — the **Bayes classifier** —
assigns each $x_0$ to the class $j$ that maximizes
$\Pr(Y = j \mid X = x_0)$. It's a gold standard we can't compute directly
(we don't know the true conditional distribution), but we can approximate it.
:::

## The floor, again: the Bayes error rate

Even the Bayes classifier isn't perfect — *best possible* still makes errors
wherever the classes genuinely overlap:

$$\text{Bayes error rate} = 1 - E\left(\max_j \Pr(Y = j \mid X)\right)$$

::: {.fragment}
- That error is built into the **problem**, not the method — it's the
  classification twin of the [irreducible error]{.hl} from the regression
  setting.
- Every real classifier (KNN, next) is judged by how close it gets to this
  floor — never by whether it reaches zero.
:::

## K-Nearest Neighbors

**KNN** approximates the Bayes classifier non-parametrically: look at the
$K$ closest training points to $x_0$ and vote.

$$\Pr(Y = j \mid X = x_0) \approx \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_0} I(y_i = j)$$

::: {.fragment}
Small $K$ → flexible, low bias, high variance. Large $K$ → the opposite.
Same trade-off, new clothes.
:::

## KNN in action ($K=3$)

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig2-14-knn-k3.png" alt=""></div>
```

Left: the 3 nearest neighbors of the test point (✕) vote — 2 blue beats 1
orange. Right: repeating this at every point traces out the decision
boundary.

## Choosing $K$ *is* choosing flexibility

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig2-16-knn-k1-vs-k100.png" alt=""></div>
```

$K=1$ (left) chases every training point — low bias, high variance.
$K=100$ (right) barely bends — high bias, low variance. The purple dashed
curve is the true (Bayes) boundary neither one quite matches.

## Where the next 7 weeks go

| Week | Focus |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Foundations** — what we just covered |
| 2 | Regression & classification: fitting $f$ to data |
| 3 | Resampling & regularization: trusting your estimate of $f$ |
| 4 | Beyond straight lines: splines, trees |
| 5–6 | Support vector machines, deep learning |
| 7 | Survival analysis, unsupervised learning, multiple testing |

Every week's lab makes the ideas from that week's slides concrete in Python.

# Getting Started {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Before next Friday

1. Read **ISLP Ch. 3–4** (`week2/ch03-04-regression-classification.pdf`) —
   Linear Regression & Classification, for Jul 17
2. If you haven't already, set up a Python environment with `jupyter`,
   `numpy`, `pandas`, `scikit-learn`, `statsmodels`, and the `ISLP` package
3. If you didn't finish running today's Ch. 2 lab — *Introduction to
   Python* — in class, catch up on it before next week's lab builds on it
4. Bring questions; we'll spend class time on discussion + the lab, not
   re-reading slides

## Questions?

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 2em;"}
[See you next Friday.]{.hl}
:::

::: notes
Leave time here for open Q&A before wrapping.
:::
